And allows us to scale this more with quality, right?

Like, we can give your team, like, here's our brand style guide.
These are, like, fonts, colors, but, like, otherwise, you can kind of create from that rather than us also needing to, like, help with building out an article's imagery.

And so if kind of where your sweet spot is being that extra arm and leg for a company, like for us, since we're seed and series A, mean, we invested in DoorDash when the company was nine weeks old.
We're often working with founders that do not have a, probably haven't hired their first marketer, probably don't have a team yet.
So the last thing they want is like to have to feed the beast of like, now you bought the pet and you've got to walk it and feed it and do all these things.
Instead of being like, I know I need this.

So rather than like needing a lot of handholding through the process, if I have that right.
Yeah, that's correct.
And so this is like for augment code, we've been working with them for a little over four weeks.
And we've done a little over 100 guides.
To be fair, most companies wouldn't be this aggressive.
They just like, they're, they're like, tell me what you need, go as aggressive as humanly possible.
We're battling like cursor and others, right?
Like, and so, but then the results hopefully speak for themselves.
Like we've essentially like 5x their blog traffic now.
And we're publishing under slash guides.
And, and you can kind of see like, you know, how much they're, and it's driving actually like really good conversion rate about like 2.3% average conversion rate from traffic, which is pretty good.
It's like pretty, and it's one of their top channels as well.
And then like, they're also ranking or showing up really well in the AI answers as well.
And so a lot of this is like kind of topics that would require a lot of kind of like nuanced things.
So for instance, like this one is for Biologica.

Kind using like the Wikipedia model of like, we don't just create the article, but making sure that it's like vetted sources that you're citing so that it's more.
Yeah, like so we have these agentic workflows under the hood.
Normally, I don't even show this, but it's like that's generating these research plans based on a lot of the context we have on the company that we built.
And then doing deep research on those based on like the source requirements.
And then we use XAI, Tavli, Perplexity, a bunch of different APIs that to go.
But like really, really deep.
And the good thing with it, and then like there's a bunch of additional workflows.

Like this one, it looks into like how to integrate the product into it, the writing guidelines, and even annotates the medical claims so that the chief medical officer can more easily review the content.
And then our editors have access to this assistant here that's kind of like a cursor-like experience that allows them to then like be able to kind of use this.
And kind of like, for instance, like expand on this and be able to work with this assistant and be able to edit the content as well, right?

Like, so essentially like what we're building internally is kind of this platform that gives our editors and our strategists like the tools to be able to like be more efficient and more productive and achieve quality at scale.
And, and, but then what's the goal that the better this gets, the more this is customers can have access to this and, and.
And kind of operate this as well, right?
Like, so, and a lot of why this, we're able to scale this stuff is because of these artifacts that we generate for customers as well.
Like this one is like an ingredient list, for example.
They can be very custom artifacts that we generate, you know, for customers that can be pulled.
We also have like a knowledge base that can pull like different sources.
And then a lot of this is like kind of the editorial pipelines, right?
Like that, that kind of help us scale this, you know?
So that's kind of like the high level.
And so in some ways, it's kind of like by working with your team, if it's one of our companies or something like that, the ability to say, hey, you don't need a content person.
You don't need to have an editor.
You don't need to have someone on SEO.

If you know you have a need to be more out there with content and you know vis-a-vis having more content out there is going to help you rank better, whether it's on Google or ChatGPT, your team can kind of bolt on.
And it's not...

Not so much like you're going to sit down and need to do interviews with all the founders about the topics, but rather can go say, probably need some information and like help me understand kind of what that process would look like.

A few weeks of kind of soul searching, deep diving of like, tell me about your company and the things we need to know, then go off, kind of do that research, find the sweet spots for avenues to build out content, then kind of get the green light to go do the content, do it.
And then it's almost more of an editorial, like something that they can review and say, does this look okay?
Is this content okay by that founder rather than the founder needing to like sit down, tell you what articles to write.
These are the areas of expertise, but yeah, let me know if I've got that right.
like what that process, that's definitely spot on for, I would say like 60, 70% of engagements.
And the reason I say that is because like, there's a wide spectrum, right?
Like, so for instance, like for...
Like Ramp, we've done things like their vendor catalog, which design built this and scale this out, right?
So things like this look different, but then for the majority of customers is exactly what you just mentioned.
Like for instance, for town, like we've done their entire blog section.
So every single piece of content here, and it's very specific to like the, like the, we call the like tax advice and things are very critical to their business.
And so you can't get, you know, tax advice wrong if what you're selling to customers is like taxes for SMBs, right?
Like, and so it's very nuanced research, fact check, and things like that.
And so, and then I would say like for the kind of companies that are probably the best for are ones that have some level of confidence in their product market fit.
Uh, because if their audience changes, you know, like all wasted, they usually have a...
Slightly more mature website, meaning they didn't just launch their website last week.
It's not just like the founding team faces and like a logo.
Like a one-page website.

And the main reason for that is like, look, if you're a seed stage company, a really early stage company, you just launched your website, you have zero content, you have no empathy for how hard it is to do content, and you haven't explored any other channels, and you don't even have a first customer, like, probably too soon.
Too soon.

Usually, like, we have worked with customers that didn't have a blog or didn't have to do any content at all before, but then those are the ones that they're like season operators that know they need content because of their category and or have validated other channels.
Like, hey, Outbound is humming, you know, and we're good, and we know we need to invest in this for the long term, right?
Like, and the reason for that is like content, even AEO, right?
Like, it's not like a, you do a thing today, and then tomorrow you get full ROI, and it's like an ad, right?
Like, you put $10 in.
And you got X impressions and that's it.
Like, you know, it's not like that.
It's kind of, it's compounding, but it does take a little while to see the signals if you're really early on and your site is like brand new and your brand is unknown.

It's not for a first time founder who's gotten their first seed round of funding that has no idea what marketing really even is and magically thinks their product's just going to sell itself, like probably not a good fit.
But for a series ABC that's starting to think about building out a marketing team, knows that they need to have this, but hasn't just has other hires they need to make or is just on the sales front.

Like you're doing five things a month and they're like, this is working, but we need to do 20 things a month and with higher quality and we need to refresh all this content and we need to do this, need to do that.
And, you know, like those are the types of companies and, and yeah, like, so, but, but it's, it's.
And.
And on the VC side, although we haven't done it, like, it'll be really cool.
Like, what we have done is, like, thought leadership.
So for hypergrowth, I think you guys know hypergrowth partners.
They're kind of like a go-to-market advisory type of thing with G and Gonto.
So their sub stack is power.
Yes, he's worked with a couple of our companies, Vercel and...
Clerk.
Clerk, yes.
Yeah, yeah.
And so, like, we run his G's sub stack, for example, you know?
And so if you go to some of the more recent stuff he's posted there, like, here's a good example is, like, this one as well.
So it's the kind of content that you would do more as a VC, you know?
Cool.
And then do you guys typically have a recommendation on, I mean, a lot of people have their existing content to date on a media...
Or hosted elsewhere other than their website?
Like, you a strong proponent of no, even like, always be on your website?
Like, where, where is your take on?
It's not one size fits all.
Like, for instance, like with G, it's like, hey, you're not going to go for like long tail SEO.
You're, you know, like, we wouldn't recommend that for VC, you know, unless they're like pretty massive.

And, you know, but, but I mean, if you want to tap into an ecosystem, like Substack has an ecosystem, Medium has an ecosystem, Beehive has an ecosystem, and it can be more of a subscriber, and you can build distribution on top of it.
And you're doing more thought leadership, opinion pieces, things like that.
That's great.
But for most more mature companies that want to scale to hundreds of millions of dollars in revenue, eventually, you know, that's not, that can't be the only thing they do, you know?
Yeah, so they need to own and be the best answer to people's questions.
Along the entire journey, right?
And even as a VC and things like that, like you have like the unusual venture has like the field guides, know, first round has done a really great job with like a lot of their content.
So there's like a lot, like if you're a founder and trying to figure out, hey, how do I calculate GRR or, you know, magic number?
What's a good magic number?
What, you know, is it bad if the magic number is really high?
Like, what does that mean?
You know what mean?
Like there's things, there are pockets that you can go and kind of because they're the resource for like founders, for example.

Like, so that's why we always take an audience first approach because once you know what your audience is, then you can figure out what are all the resources and things that you can do to help them do their jobs better and be successful, you know?
So you'll even help with the triangulation of that of should we be on Substack?
Should we take, use Medium if we're thinking about porting it over?
Or should we, like, you can even kind of help them a little bit with some of that decision making too in terms of what audience is most...
important to them and where they might want to be.
Yeah, yeah, exactly.
I think what we try to stay away from is like, we're not their web dev and design shop, you know, they're not going to own their website.
And usually like, if they don't even have a place to publish, we'll usually have a place to publish first, but we can do more strategy on where or which kind of which levers to be pulling.
And then how do you guys typically work with companies?
Is it like on a retainer basis?
Is it you buy a chunk of articles?
I'm sorry, I got to run to this meeting.
But Tyler can stay on and kind of go through some of those details if that's okay with you.
Yeah, I've actually got a one too.
So I've got like 60 seconds as well.
But yeah, I'll just tell you that the quick TLDR is like, we do what's called a strategy sprint, which is eight weeks.
And as part of that strategy sprint, it's like we're calibrating the strategy, we're calibrating the definition of done.
And kind of like scoping out what quality looks like.
And then from there, goes into a year contract that's like on a retainer basis and essentially like a monthly retainer.
Most of our customers are around like 15,000 a month.
Cool.
Awesome.
Well, great meeting you guys.
Like I said, I'm in like my super early like researching stages.
I appreciate the time and yeah, just great to learn more.
Yeah, absolutely.
We'll, we'll talk soon, hopefully.
All right.
Bye.
Yeah.
Bye, Krista.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Introduce GrowthX Labs' AI-driven content and SEO services to CRV for potential collaboration and portfolio company recommendations.](https://fathom.video/share/wy6Wr7a663boDSKWYbDJRVPneKJVC_3z?tab=summary&timestamp=150.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [GrowthX Labs offers end-to-end content strategy, creation, and optimization services, including SEO and AI-optimized content.](https://fathom.video/share/wy6Wr7a663boDSKWYbDJRVPneKJVC_3z?tab=summary&timestamp=673.0)
  - [Their approach is best suited for companies with some product-market fit and a mature website, typically Series A and beyond.](https://fathom.video/share/wy6Wr7a663boDSKWYbDJRVPneKJVC_3z?tab=summary&timestamp=1301.0)
  - [GrowthX uses AI-driven workflows and human expertise to produce high-quality, scalable content at competitive rates (\~$0.10/word).](https://fathom.video/share/wy6Wr7a663boDSKWYbDJRVPneKJVC_3z?tab=summary&timestamp=856.0)
  - [Engagement typically starts with an 8-week strategy sprint, followed by a year-long retainer (often around $15,000/month).](https://fathom.video/share/wy6Wr7a663boDSKWYbDJRVPneKJVC_3z?tab=summary&timestamp=1648.0)

## Topics

### GrowthX Labs Overview

  - [Founded over a year ago, now at 75 employees and $12 million in revenue](https://fathom.video/share/wy6Wr7a663boDSKWYbDJRVPneKJVC_3z?tab=summary&timestamp=285.0)
  - [Serving 60 customers, including notable names like Webflow, Lovable, and CodeRabbit (a CRV portfolio company)](https://fathom.video/share/wy6Wr7a663boDSKWYbDJRVPneKJVC_3z?tab=summary&timestamp=313.0)
  - [Offers end-to-end content services: strategy, AI workflows, expert oversight, and execution](https://fathom.video/share/wy6Wr7a663boDSKWYbDJRVPneKJVC_3z?tab=summary&timestamp=673.0)
  - [Positions as a full content team replacement, handling everything from ideation to publication and measurement](https://fathom.video/share/wy6Wr7a663boDSKWYbDJRVPneKJVC_3z?tab=summary&timestamp=840.0)

### AI-Driven Content Approach

  - [Uses AI for research, drafting, fact-checking, and even image creation](https://fathom.video/share/wy6Wr7a663boDSKWYbDJRVPneKJVC_3z?tab=summary&timestamp=771.0)
  - [Implements agentic workflows for scalable, high-quality content production](https://fathom.video/share/wy6Wr7a663boDSKWYbDJRVPneKJVC_3z?tab=summary&timestamp=771.0)
  - [Focuses on both SEO and AI-answer optimization (e.g., for ChatGPT visibility)](https://fathom.video/share/wy6Wr7a663boDSKWYbDJRVPneKJVC_3z?tab=summary&timestamp=660.0)
  - [Generates custom artifacts and knowledge bases for each client to ensure contextual accuracy](https://fathom.video/share/wy6Wr7a663boDSKWYbDJRVPneKJVC_3z?tab=summary&timestamp=1142.0)
